retrieved_docs.title: Ethambutol -- Administration -- Initial treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Initial treatment for patients who have not received previous tuberculosis therapy should be administered with 15 mg/kg of body weight every 24 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Antitubercular Medications -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the increased risk of infection in patients prescribed brodalumab, all patients require screening for tuberculosis (TB) before initiation of the medication. If a patient has an active TB infection, the clinician should not administer the drug. For patients with latent TB or a history of latent or active TB with unconfirmed treatment, therapy for TB infection should start before the initiation of brodalumab, as there is a risk for latent TB reactivation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Before initiating treatment with upadacitinib, a negative tuberculosis (TB) test is required as it may reactivate latent TB infection. During treatment with upadacitinib, patients should be routinely tested for TB. A complete blood count (CBC) must be monitored before starting therapy and routinely after that; initiating upadacitinib is not recommended for subjects with an absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) below 500 cells/mm³ or an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) below 1,000 cells/mm³. Reports of lymphopenia, neutropenia, and anemia exist. Liver function tests (LFTs) should be checked before beginning therapy and regularly after that, as patients with severe hepatic impairment are not advised to start treatment. [27] The lipid panel should also be monitored 12 weeks after starting treatment, as increases in total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol may occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Treatment / Management -- Bacille Calmette-Guerin Vaccine
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, this clinical situation cannot be addressed with absolute certainty. The guidelines approach this scenario somewhat indirectly and state the following:  "Performing a second diagnostic test when the initial test is negative is one strategy to increase sensitivity. While this strategy to increase sensitivity may reduce the specificity of diagnostic testing, this may be an acceptable tradeoff in situations in which it is determined that the consequences of missing latent TB infection (ie, not treating individuals who may benefit from therapy) exceed the consequences of inappropriate therapy (ie, hepatotoxicity)." In this complicated situation, the consensus among most experts is to begin a regimen of TB preventive therapy several weeks before the start of the immunosuppressing intervention. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Deucravacitinib is contraindicated in patients with active TB. Starting therapy for latent TB before administering deucravacitinib is recommended. If the patient has a medical history of TB, latent or active, and an adequate course of therapy cannot be confirmed, initiating anti-TB treatment before administering deucravacitinib may be considered. Patients on deucravacitinib treatment should be monitored for signs and symptoms of active TB during and after treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management -- Drug-Resistant Central Nervous System Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: The guidelines for managing drug-resistant CNS-TB are similar to those for PTB. The key principle involves never adding a single drug to a failing regimen. Management is challenging due to the huge number of drugs with overlapping adverse drug reaction profiles. The newer agents, bedaquiline and delamanid, received accelerated approvals for PTB but not CNS-TB. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lung Tuberculosis(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of tuberculosis in HIV-positive patients constitutes a unique challenge due to potential drug interaction with antiretroviral therapy. In general, it is still recommended to use the same duration of treatment for both intensive and continuation phases (two and four months) in HIV-positive patients who are infected with drug-susceptible tuberculosis. The exception is an unusual situation when the patient is not receiving antiretroviral therapy. In this situation, it is suggested that the continuation phase is continued for 3 additional months. [59] The concomitant use of trimoxazole and anti-tuberculous drugs has been shown to improve outcomes in HIV patients with active tuberculosis, particularly those with a CD4 count below 200 cells/µL. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Ruxolitinib -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients starting or continuing ruxolitinib need to be monitored in multiple domains. Due to the increased risk of infection, all patients require monitoring for signs of infection throughout treatment while taking ruxolitinib. Studies have shown increased rates of urinary tract infections, hepatitis, pneumonia, tuberculosis, and herpes zoster infection; therefore, clinicians should keep this in mind, and patients should be screened for signs of infection. [19] Clinicians should perform tuberculosis screening in high-risk patients to investigate for any active or latent tuberculosis infection prior to starting ruxolitinib treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacillus Calmette Guerin -- Contraindications -- Immunocompromised patients
retrieved_docs.content: BCG is a live vaccine and should not be given to: Immunocompromised persons with congenital immunodeficiency, HIV infection, malignancy, or those taking immunosuppressive drugs such as tumor necrosis factor-alpha blockers and corticosteroids Adults with HIV infection living in areas of low TB prevalence

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Before initiating treatment with upadacitinib, it is necessary to ensure that immunizations are up-to-date and to assess for active or latent tuberculosis (TB). Additional evaluations include liver function, viral hepatitis panel, and pregnancy status in females of childbearing age. Concomitant administration of upadacitinib with other JAK inhibitors, biologic DMARD agents, or potent immunosuppressants such as azathioprine and cyclosporine is not recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Longer treatment periods are advisable for children, immunocompromised individuals, patients with a slow clinical response, and the presence of TBM, TB lymphadenitis, or skeletal TB. The generally recommended minimum therapeutic duration is 9 months for skeletal TB and 12 months for TBM. [57] [58] [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The protective efficacy of established regimens for latent TB ranges from 60% to 90% for up to 19 years. [67] In persons with HIV infection in areas with a high incidence of tuberculosis, the optimal duration of therapy for latent TB infection is not well established due to the risks of repeated or ongoing exposure and host immune status changes. [68] [69]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-1–Associated Opportunistic Infections -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumocystis jirovecii: All HIV positive patients, including pregnant women, must be started on TMP-SMX for chemoprophylaxis when their CD4+ count is less than 200 cells/mm^3. Alternative prophylactic medications include dapsone, dapsone plus pyrimethamine plus leucovorin, atovaquone and aerosolized pentamidine. PCP is treated with TMP-SMZ which is the drug of choice. Other drugs include dapsone, TMP, clindamycin plus primaquine or IV pentamidine, atovaquone suspension. Aerosolized pentamidine is not used to treat PCP. Recommended duration of therapy is 21 days. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lung Tuberculosis(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Drug-resistant Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple second-line drugs for MDR, but unfortunately, the cure rate is much lower than drug-susceptible tuberculosis. The global cure rate is variable between countries. Surveys reported cure rates between 20 to 48%. [67] As a rule, any medication that was used in first-line regimens should be excluded from second-line therapy. DST and history should guide second-line therapy. Classically the regimen consists of four drugs. The first step is to choose an injectable medication. Examples include kanamycin and capreomycin. Adding a fluoroquinolone such as moxifloxacin or levofloxacin is the next step. The third choice is one of these options: ethionamide, cycloserine, or para-aminosalicylic acid. The last step is to add either PZA or EMB to the regimen. [70] The duration of treatment of MDR-TB is a minimum of 20 months and for an additional 18 months after being culture negative.

retrieved_docs.title: Deucravacitinib -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculosis: Deucravacitinib is a systemic therapy that inhibits tyrosine kinase 2, a member of the Jak family. Before initiating therapy with deucravacitinib, patients must be evaluated for latent and active tuberculosis (TB). [24] A blood-based interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) can detect mycobacterium tuberculosis infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1121
retrieved_docs.content: Amfotericin B is classified as a member of the group “Antibiotics for systemic use,” indicating that it is administered systemically to treat infections. Within this therapeutic category, systemic antibiotics also encompass tuberculostatic agents, which are used specifically against Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Amfotericin B may treat diseases of white blood cells, suggesting efficacy in conditions that involve leukocyte pathology. Meropenem anhydrous, another agent used systemically, belongs to the structural class of carbapenems, a subgroup of β‑lactam antibiotics known for broad‑spectrum activity. Like amfotericin B, meropenem anhydrous may also be employed to treat diseases of white blood cells. Thus, both amfotericin B and meropenem anhydrous are systemic antimicrobial drugs, each belonging to distinct structural classes (polyene antifungal versus carbapenem β‑lactam) but sharing the therapeutic indication of managing disorders affecting white blood cells, while the broader category of systemic antibiotics further includes tuberculostatic medications for tuberculosis treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: viral integration. Rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, and methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection. Needle sharing is identified as a risk factor for HIV acquisition. Systemic antimycotics are represented in the ATC subgroup “Other antimycotics for systemic use,” which includes the echinocandin lipopeptides kaspofungin and micafungin. Gemifloxacin is used to treat coccidioidal pneumonia, a fungal lung infection that may arise as a complication of influenza. Clinically, ribavirin is used for viral infection prevention, remdesivir for treatment of viral infections, zanamivir for influenza prophylaxis, and both brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for smallpox. Hierarchically, “viral infection” is the parent category for smallpox; “influenza” (English) is the parent term for its Arabic equivalent (الإنفلونزا) and for coccidioidal pneumonia as a child condition. These hierarchical and mechanistic relationships guide therapeutic selection

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: related nucleoside analogues include abacavir and its fixed‑dose combinations (related to dideoxyadenosine) and brivudine (a bromodeoxyuridine analogue). Monoclonal antibodies in this group are abciximab and ibalizumab. Remdesivir is indicated for treatment of viral infections, while ribavirin may be employed for viral infection prophylaxis. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are used in the treatment of smallpox. Zanamivir is employed for influenza prophylaxis; influenza, an acute respiratory viral infection, can present as pneumonia with nodular infiltrates, a condition also treatable with the antibacterial gemifloxacin. In the antifungal domain, the ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” includes the echinocandin agents caspofungin (a lipopeptide) and micafungin. These entities illustrate how antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drugs are organized into mechanistic ATC classes—direct‑acting antivirals, other antivirals, entry inhibitors, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics for systemic use encompass a group of tuberculostatic antibiotics, referred to in Czech as “antibiotika tuberkulostatická.” This subclass of systemic antibiotics is specifically employed to inhibit the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is often used in multidrug‑resistant or refractory tuberculosis regimens. Within the tuberculostatic category, both cykloserin (cycloserine) and viomycin are recognized agents; each exemplifies a distinct chemical type that shares the common therapeutic purpose of tuberculostatic activity. By incorporating cykloserin and viomycin, the broader class of antibiotics for systemic use provides clinicians with alternative options for targeting persistent mycobacterial infections, thereby enhancing the flexibility and effectiveness of tuberculosis treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: brivudine (a bromodeoxyuridine analogue) and remdesivir; brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for smallpox, and bulevirtide is described as a lipopeptide. Remdesivir may treat viral infection, while ribavirin may be used for viral infection prevention. In the respiratory domain, gemifloxacin treats a form of pneumonia that can arise as a complication of influenza, and zanamivir may be used to prevent influenza. Systemic antifungal therapy is represented by the “Other antimycotics for systemic use” group, which includes the echinocandins caspofungin (a lipopeptide‑derived agent) and micafungin. Monoclonal antibody biologics in this community include t anezumab, tecovirimat, ibalizumab and abciximab. A clinically important drug interaction is the contraindication of the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole with the NNRTI rilpivirine. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how antiviral drugs, nucleoside analogues, monoclonal antibodies, lipopeptides and other direct‑acting

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: therapies. Clinically, gemifloxacin may treat granulomatous pneumonia, a pulmonary complication that can follow influenza infection; zanamivir provides prophylaxis against influenza. Smallpox, an orthopoxvirus disease, can be treated with brincidofovir or tecovirimat. Together, these classifications, molecular targets, mechanisms of action, contraindications, and formulation details constitute an integrated framework for antiviral therapy, anticoagulation management, antifungal treatment, and peptide‑based diagnostic imaging in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 5
retrieved_docs.content: includes brincidofovir, brivudine (chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine) and remdesivir; remdesivir is indicated for viral infections and ribavirin may be used for prophylaxis. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat have activity against smallpox. The ATC “Other antivirals” group gathers bulevirtide (a lipopeptidy scaffold), enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, tecovirimat and tiloron. Both abciximab and ibalizumab belong to the monoclonal antibody class. Neuraminidase inhibitors such as zanamivir constitute a distinct antiviral class targeting influenza and are not employed in HIV management; zanamivir can prevent influenza, a viral disease that may lead to complications such as coccidioidal pneumonia, which can be treated with the antibiotic gemifloxacin. Within the antifungal ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use,” the echinocandin drugs kaspofungin (a lipopeptidy compound) and micafungin are listed. HIV infection is categorized as a viral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: group and is used to treat viral infection; ribavirin, another nucleoside analogue, is noted for preventing viral infection. The ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” comprises the lipopeptide antifungals kaspofungin and micafungin. “Other antivirals in ATC” include bulevirtide (a lipopeptidy compound), enfuvirtide, maraviroc, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, tecovirimat, and tiloron. Bulevirtide’s lipopeptidy structure links it to other lipopeptide agents. Ibalizumab and abciximab are monoclonal antibodies, the latter primarily serving as an antiplatelet agent. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat exhibit activity against smallpox (الجدري), a viral infection. These shared scaffolds and ATC classifications illustrate how chemical structure, mechanism of action, and therapeutic indication intersect, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate antiviral, antifungal, or antibody‑based therapies while recognizing important contraindications and epidemiologic risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 0 -- Community 7
retrieved_docs.content: include the fluoroquinolone gemifloxacin for cocci‑related pneumonia, and the monoclonal antibody abciximab for thrombotic conditions. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are antiviral agents indicated for smallpox, and zanamivir is used prophylactically against influenza, a viral respiratory illness that can progress to pneumonia with nodular infiltrates. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured taxonomy of antiviral, antibacterial, antifungal, anticoagulant, radiopharmaceutical and immunomodulatory agents organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, thereby supporting precise prescribing, safety monitoring, and diagnostic use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 0 -- Community 10
retrieved_docs.content: to bromodeoxyuridine and associated with the remdesivir family. Brivudine, abacavir and fixed‑dose combinations (abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine; abacavir / lamivudine) are noted as analogues of dideoxyadenosine. Remdesivir is indicated for viral infection treatment; ribavirin for viral infection prevention; zanamivir for influenza prophylaxis, which may be complicated by secondary mycobacterial pneumonia treatable with gemifloxacin. Brincidofovir is indicated for smallpox, and tecovirimat is also employed to treat smallpox, confirming its role against viral infections. A drug‑interaction warning is recorded between pantoprazole and the NNRTI rilpivirine, underscoring the need for careful medication reconciliation. Monoclonal antibodies serve therapeutic roles (abciximab, ibalizumab) and diagnostic roles (arcitumomab). Collectively, peptide‑derived scaffolds, lipopeptidy backbones, nucleoside/nucleotide analogues, monoclonal antibody platforms and radiometal labeling converge on

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1569
retrieved_docs.content: Doravirine, a non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in antiretroviral therapy, is contraindicated when co‑administered with rifapentine, a rifamycin antibiotic employed for tuberculosis treatment and prophylaxis; the interaction between these agents can significantly reduce doravirine exposure, making simultaneous use unsafe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: and nucleotide analogues excluded from the NRTI class—brincidofovir, brivudine (a bromodeoxyuridine analogue) and remdesivir—are employed against a range of viral infections; remdesivir is used for general viral infections, ribavirin for viral prophylaxis, and brincidofovir and tecovirimat for smallpox. Neuraminidase inhibitors, a subclass of DAAs, are exemplified by zanamivir for influenza treatment and prevention, and influenza may be complicated by secondary bacterial pneumonia that can be managed with the antibiotic gemifloxacin. Systemic antifungal therapy is represented by caspofungin and micafungin, both members of the “Other antimycotics for systemic use” ATC group; caspofungin is a lipopeptide, linking it structurally to bulevirtide, which is derived from a lipopeptidy scaffold. The “Other antivirals in ATC” category includes bulevirtide, enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab (a monoclonal antibody), tecovirimat and tiloron, reflecting antiviral agents

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2031
retrieved_docs.content: Antimykobakteriální látky are classified within the therapeutic class kapreomycinsulfát, and they include the drugs specifically used for the treatment of tuberculosis. This indicates that kapreomycinsulfát defines the pharmacologic category of these antimycobacterial agents, while the subset of drugs for tuberculosis is a specialized group that falls under the broader category of antimykobakteriální látky, highlighting their role in managing mycobacterial infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2506
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics for systemic use are linked to the class of tuberculostatic antibiotics (antibiotika tuberculostatická), indicating that systemic antimicrobial therapy includes agents specifically designed to inhibit the growth of Mycobacterium tuberculosis. This relationship highlights that tuberculostatic antibiotics are a subset of systemic antibiotics used in the treatment of tuberculosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: maribavir, moroxydine, ribavirin (preventive use), and zanamivir (influenza prophylaxis). Monoclonal antibodies appear both as antiviral biologics (tanezumab, ibalizumab) and as non‑antiviral agents (abciximab). Anti‑HIV agents are linked epidemiologically to the risk factor “shared needle use” and are divided into preventive (dolutegravir, efavirenz, emtricitabine, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine) and therapeutic (cabotegravir, fostemsavir, maraviroc) categories. Beyond antivirals, the ATC “Other antimycotics for systemic use” includes the echinocandins kaspofungin and micafungin, while “Other chemotherapeutics” lists ingenol mebutate and defines a subclass of topical chemotherapeutics. Gemifloxacin is noted for the management of coccidioidal pneumonia, and multilingual disease nomenclature connects the Arabic term الإنفلونزا (influenza) as a child concept of coccidioidal pneumonia. Collectively, these entities

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 229
retrieved_docs.content: strongly associated with needle sharing; a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents (NRTIs, NNRTIs, integrase inhibitors and cyclic amines) provides options for both prophylaxis and early therapeutic regimens. Remdesivir is indicated for the treatment of viral infections. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are antiviral medications used to treat smallpox. Zanamivir is employed for influenza prophylaxis. Gemifloxacin, a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is used to manage coccidioidal pneumonia—a fungal lung infection that can arise as a secondary complication of influenza. Collectively, this network of antiviral, antiretroviral, ophthalmologic and antibacterial agents illustrates a pharmacologic strategy that interferes with viral replication at multiple enzymatic steps while also addressing associated bacterial or fungal complications, thereby supporting tailored patient care.
